items.title: Kontext statt Etikett
items.digest: Der Eintrag ordnet das Thema ueber datierte Ereignisse, benannte Akteure und redaktionellen Kontext ein, damit berichtete Fakten von Interpretation getrennt bleiben. Statt kategorischer Etiketten setzt der Text auf Verhaeltnis, Praezision und Nachpruefbarkeit, besonders in Phasen hoher Unsicherheit. Statt mit pauschalen Etiketten zu arbeiten, rekonstruiert der Text den Fall ueber benannte Akteure, zeitliche Abfolge und institutionellen Publikationskontext.

items.title: "Auschwitz umgekehrt": Interview ueber Kriegssprache und Zukunft

Maerz 2026 dokumentiert ein polemisch gefuehrtes Interview, in dem Ilia Klishin ueber historische Vergleiche, Kriegssprache und geopolitische Abhaengigkeiten spricht. Ausgangspunkt ist die provokative Formulierung "Auschwitz umgekehrt", mit der die rhetorische Eskalation im oeffentlichen Diskurs sichtbar gemacht wird.

items.title: Wachsen Revolutionen aus sozialen Netzwerken?
items.digest: Dieser Carnegie-Text stellt das Klischee infrage, soziale Netzwerke wuerden direkt Revolutionen ausloesen. Plattformen koennen Sichtbarkeit, Koordination und emotionale Dynamik beschleunigen, ersetzen aber weder Institutionen noch Organisation noch politische Gelegenheitsstrukturen. An post-sowjetischen Beispielen trennt der Beitrag Kommunikationseffekte von kausalen Aussagen ueber Regimewandel und warnt vor technologischem Determinismus.

items.title: Wogegen richtete sich Moldaus zweiter Maidan?
items.digest: Diese Carnegie-Analyse untersucht erneute Protestmobilisierung in Moldau und fragt, warum Frustration nach frueheren Wahldurchbruechen zurueckkehrte. Der Beitrag verfolgt die Luecke zwischen Reformversprechen und Alltagsergebnissen und zeigt, wie wiederholte Enttaeuschung politische Strassenmobilisierung reaktivieren kann, auch ohne institutionellen Bruch. Der Eintrag dient als regionaler Fall zu wiederkehrender Protestdynamik im postsowjetischen Raum.

items.title: Privater Autoritarismus: Wie Facebook und Google die Demokratie bedrohen
items.digest: Dieser Carnegie-Text argumentiert, dass grosse private Plattformen politische Macht ohne gleichwertige demokratische Rechenschaft aufgebaut haben. Er zeigt, wie Ranking-Algorithmen, Moderationsregeln und intransparente Produktentscheidungen oeffentliche Debatten praegen koennen, teils so stark wie staatliche Medienkontrolle. Der Eintrag wird als fruehe Rahmung von Plattformmacht als Governance-Problem gefuehrt.

items.title: Putin-Macron talks and the Russia expertise gap
items.digest: In this Feb. 14, 2022 Moscow Times opinion piece, Ilya Klishin argues that a small factual error in foreign reporting around Putin-Macron talks exposed a larger expertise problem in commentary about Russia. The trigger is a repeated claim that Putin quoted a specific punk song after his meeting with Emmanuel Macron.
items.quote: "every word spoken carries weight" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: A small factual mistake in coverage of the Putin-Macron meeting became, for Klishin, a sign of a larger problem: too much commentary on Russia is built on weak expertise. The piece argues that even minor errors can reveal how shallow the broader analytical frame has become.
quotes: "every word spoken carries weight" - The Moscow Times
"the dissemination of rumors, the fanning of hysteria" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Mass protests: lessons from a decade
items.digest: This essay reevaluates the 2011 Bolotnaya protests not as a tactical failure, but as a structural inevitability. Framing the defeat as a Hegelian catalyst, it argues the subsequent crackdown inadvertently served as a "civil society university," transforming a naive public into a deeply politicized society primed for an unpredictable historical synthesis.
items.quote: "those demonstrations ended in defeat" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Ten years after Bolotnaya, Klishin argues that the protest wave should not be read simply as a defeat. Its longer effect was political learning: a generation that entered the streets naively left with a deeper understanding of the state, repression, and civic action.
quotes: "those demonstrations ended in defeat" - The Moscow Times
"The Kremlin propaganda machine has waged a war" - The Moscow Times
"whether protesters ever had any chance at all of winning" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Mandatory vaccination and public resistance
items.digest: In this June 18, 2021 Moscow Times commentary, Klishin examines why Russia's vaccination rollout stalled even though domestic vaccines were available, free, and widely distributed. The article contrasts two communication realities: high state capacity for political messaging and low persuasive impact in public-health trust building. It describes a social split in vaccine uptake, noting stronger willingness among younger urban groups and deeper skepticism among audiences usually aligned with state narratives.
items.quote: "People simply don’t want to get vaccinated!" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Holod interview with Ilya Klishin
items.digest: This July 13, 2023 interview in Holod gives an operational view of how the Volna media project was launched and scaled for Russian-speaking relocants in Lithuania, Latvia, Germany, and other destinations. Klishin describes the product logic as service-first: daily local digests, practical orientation, and lower-anxiety information flow for people adapting to new legal and social environments. The conversation explains why Volna did not copy high-frequency conflict feeds from major exile outlets; instead, it prioritized usable local context, from transport and documents to civic events, while keeping an explicit anti-war position.
items.quote: "Respect for the host country" - Holod

items.title: Likes Go West
items.digest:
In a prescient May 2014 column for Vedomosti-published more than two full years before the 2016 US presidential election and the ensuing global scandal over Russian cyber interference-Klishin exposed the Kremlin’s nascent digital operations targeting Western democracies. Relying on exclusive sources close to the Presidential Administration, leaked files from "Anonymous International," and insights from European media executives, the piece detailed the strategic pivot of domestic troll farms toward American and European audiences.

Klishin dismantled the emerging operational mindset of these state-sponsored networks. He documented their early structural and financial foundations, revealing how they tested synthetic engagement, studied the pre-moderation algorithms of major outlets like The Huffington Post and Politico, and refined their use of provocative, polarized messaging. This investigation not only mapped the exact infrastructure of the new "foreign departments" but accurately predicted the weaponization of social media long before coordinated online manipulation became a defining issue of global politics.
items.quote: "Work aimed at the West was only beginning" - Vedomosti
items.summary:
In a prescient May 2014 column for Vedomosti-published more than two full years before the 2016 US presidential election and the ensuing global scandal over Russian cyber interference-Klishin exposed the Kremlin’s nascent digital operations targeting Western democracies. Klishin dismantled the emerging operational mindset of these state-sponsored networks. He documented their early structural and financial foundations, revealing how they tested synthetic engagement, studied the pre-moderation algorithms of major outlets like The Huffington Post and Politico, and refined their use of provocative, polarized messaging. This investigation not only mapped the exact infrastructure of the new "foreign departments" but accurately predicted the weaponization of social media long before coordinated online manipulation became a defining issue of global politics.

items.title: TV Rain launched "Our Soldiers" civilian investigation
items.digest: This August 2014 TV Rain project page introduces "Our Soldiers," a public-facing investigation into reports of Russian servicemen killed, missing, or captured near the Ukrainian border during a period of official denials. The framing is explicit: the goal is not rhetorical escalation but factual clarification of identities, numbers, and circumstances. The page states that if authorities withhold information, independent verification must be built from open submissions, witness reports, and case-level documentation.
items.quote: "All we want is to understand what happened" - TV Rain
items.summary: In 2014, the project set out to verify something the state preferred to leave blurred: whether Russian servicemen were being killed, captured, or disappearing near the Ukrainian border. The emphasis was on names, dates, and circumstances rather than on rhetoric.

items.title: HRW report on harassment of critics includes Klishin case context
items.digest: This 2012 Human Rights Watch report chronicles the Kremlin’s strategic shift toward targeted pressure on protest organizers. A prime example of this repressive mechanic is the documented episode involving Ilya Klishin, the creator of the "For Fair Elections" network: operatives from the anti-extremism Center "E" interrogated his parents in Tambov, threatening them with vague extremism charges. The text vividly illustrates how authorities, spooked by grassroots mobilization, began deploying psychological blackmail—cynically redirecting their strikes from metropolitan activists to their vulnerable relatives in the provinces.
items.quote: "Harassment of critics" — Human Rights Watch
items.summary: This 2012 Human Rights Watch report chronicles the Kremlin’s strategic shift toward targeted pressure on protest organizers. items.title: Bot Army: Why the Kremlin Fails at Soft Power

items.title: Coronavirus Spoils Putin's 'Forever-in-Power' Public Vote
items.digest: The column explains how the pandemic disrupted a carefully staged constitutional plebiscite. Klishin argues that the Kremlin lost timing control and faced a strategic bind where postponement and continuation both carried rising political costs.
items.quote: "Coronavirus Spoils Putin's 'Forever-in-Power' Public Vote" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: The pandemic broke the political timing on which the constitutional spectacle depended. Once that choreography failed, both delay and continuation began to carry growing costs for the Kremlin.
key_ideas: The card identifies actors, timeline, and stakes without speculative framing.
It connects the material to a broader editorial and institutional context.

items.title: How Putin Is Bankrupting the Russian Opposition
items.digest: This English column describes bankruptcy tactics as a calibrated instrument of repression. Instead of spectacular crackdowns, the state can deplete opposition capacity through legal debts, penalties, and procedural pressure while preserving formal plausibility.
items.quote: "How Putin Is Bankrupting the Russian Opposition" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Writing for an English-speaking audience, Klishin describes a method quieter than a crackdown but not less effective. Debt, penalties, and procedural pressure can drain opposition capacity while preserving a formal appearance of legality — a mechanism Western observers are less likely to recognize as repression.
key_ideas: The card identifies actors, timeline, and stakes without speculative framing.
It connects the material to a broader editorial and institutional context.

items.title: Do Revolutions Grow Out of Social Media?
items.digest: This Carnegie essay challenges the cliche that social networks directly produce revolutions. It argues that platforms can accelerate visibility, coordination, and emotional contagion, but they do not replace institutions, organization, and political opportunity structures. Using post-Soviet examples, the text separates communication effects from causal claims about regime change and warns against technological determinism in media analysis.
items.quote: "Do revolutions grow out of social media?" - Carnegie Endowment
items.summary: Social networks can accelerate visibility, coordination, and emotional contagion — but they do not produce revolutions on their own. Using post-Soviet examples, the essay separates communication effects from the political structures that make mass mobilization possible.
key_ideas: Social networks amplify mobilization but do not create political agency alone.
Institutional conditions decide whether protest momentum becomes durable change.
The essay separates media effects from direct regime-change causality claims.
quotes: "Do revolutions grow out of social media?" - Carnegie Endowment
"social networks" - Carnegie Endowment
items.value_context: Useful for separating media amplification from political causality in debates about digital protest dynamics across post-Soviet contexts.

items.title: What Sparked Moldova's Second Maidan?
items.digest: This Carnegie analysis examines renewed protest mobilization in Moldova and asks why frustration returned after earlier electoral breakthroughs. The text tracks the gap between reform promises and lived outcomes, showing how repeated cycles of disappointment can reactivate street politics even when formal institutions remain unchanged. It is included as a regional case study of post-Soviet protest recurrence and narrative fatigue.
items.quote: "Moldova's second Maidan" - Carnegie Endowment
items.summary: In Moldova, an apparent political breakthrough did not end the frustrations that had produced protest in the first place. The piece follows repeated cycles of disappointment between reform promises and lived reality, showing how the street can reopen as a political arena.
key_ideas: Electoral turnover did not eliminate structural drivers of public frustration.
Repeated unmet expectations can reactivate protest despite institutional continuity.
The case maps cyclical protest logic in post-Soviet political systems.
quotes: "second Maidan" - Carnegie Endowment
"Moldova" - Carnegie Endowment
items.value_context: Adds a comparative regional case on protest recurrence, useful for interpreting delayed political effects after apparent electoral wins.

items.title: Private Authoritarianism: How Facebook and Google Threaten Democracy
items.digest: This Carnegie essay argues that major private platforms gained political power without democratic accountability. It examines how algorithmic ranking, moderation policies, and opaque product decisions can shape public debate as strongly as state media controls. The text is included as an early framing of platform power as a governance problem rather than only a technical or market issue.
items.quote: "Private authoritarianism" - Carnegie Endowment
items.summary: This Carnegie essay argues that major private platforms gained political power without democratic accountability. It examines how algorithmic ranking, moderation policies, and opaque product decisions can shape public debate as strongly as state media controls. The text is included as an early framing of platform power as a governance problem rather than only a technical or market issue.
key_ideas: Platform design decisions can produce political effects without public oversight.
Algorithmic visibility rules act as private governance over civic discourse.
The essay reframes platform power as democratic accountability challenge.
quotes: "Private authoritarianism" - Carnegie Endowment
"Facebook and Google" - Carnegie Endowment
items.value_context: Strengthens the archive with an early argument about platform governance, accountability, and democratic risk beyond state media frameworks.

items.title: Nacen las revoluciones en las redes sociales?
items.digest: Este ensayo de Carnegie cuestiona el cliche de que las redes sociales generan revoluciones de forma directa. Las plataformas pueden acelerar visibilidad, coordinacion y contagio emocional, pero no sustituyen instituciones, organizacion ni oportunidades politicas. Con ejemplos postsovieticos, el texto separa efectos de comunicacion y causalidad politica, y advierte contra el determinismo tecnologico en el analisis publico.
items.quote: "Nacen las revoluciones en las redes sociales?" - Carnegie Endowment

Con ejemplos postsovieticos, el texto separa efectos de comunicacion y causalidad politica, y advierte contra el determinismo tecnologico en el analisis publico.
key_ideas: Las redes amplifican movilizacion, pero no crean agencia politica por si solas.
Las condiciones institucionales definen si el impulso protesta produce cambio.
El texto separa efectos mediaticos y causalidad directa de cambio politico.

items.title: Contra que se levantaba el segundo Maidan moldavo?
items.digest: Este analisis de Carnegie examina la reactivacion de protestas en Moldavia y pregunta por que regreso la frustracion tras avances electorales previos. El texto sigue la brecha entre promesas de reforma y resultados vividos, y muestra como ciclos repetidos de decepcion pueden devolver la politica a la calle incluso sin cambios institucionales. Se incluye como estudio regional sobre recurrencia protestataria postsovietica.

items.title: Autoritarismo privado: como Facebook y Google amenazan la democracia
items.digest: Este ensayo de Carnegie sostiene que las grandes plataformas privadas acumularon poder politico sin una rendicion democratica equivalente. Analiza como ranking algoritmico, politicas de moderacion y decisiones opacas de producto pueden moldear el debate publico con una fuerza comparable al control mediatico estatal. Se incluye como marco temprano que trata el poder de plataformas como problema de gobernanza.

items.title: L avenir annule: politique du present en temps de crise
items.digest: Dans cette chronique publiee par Vedomosti le 20 mai 2020, Ilia Klishin analyse le changement de temporalite politique provoque par la crise sanitaire. items.title: Les revolutions naissent-elles des reseaux sociaux?
items.digest: Cet essai de Carnegie remet en cause le cliche selon lequel les reseaux sociaux produiraient directement des revolutions. Les plateformes peuvent accelerer la visibilite, la coordination et la contagion emotionnelle, mais elles ne remplacent ni les institutions, ni l organisation, ni les fenetres politiques. En s appuyant sur des exemples post-sovietiques, le texte distingue effets de communication et causalite politique.
items.quote: "Les revolutions naissent-elles des reseaux sociaux?" - Carnegie Endowment

En s appuyant sur des exemples post-sovietiques, le texte distingue effets de communication et causalite politique.
key_ideas: Les reseaux sociaux amplifient la mobilisation mais ne creent pas l agence politique.
Les conditions institutionnelles determinent la durabilite du changement protestataire.
Le texte separe effets mediatique et causalite directe de changement de regime.

items.title: Contre quoi se dressait le deuxieme Maidan moldave?
items.digest: Cette analyse Carnegie etudie la reprise des mobilisations en Moldavie et demande pourquoi la frustration est revenue apres des avancees electorales precedentes. Le texte suit l ecart entre promesses de reforme et resultats vecus, montrant comment des cycles repetes de deception peuvent reactiver la rue meme sans changement institutionnel majeur. La carte sert de cas regional sur la recurrence protestataire postsovietique.

items.title: Autoritarisme prive : comment Facebook et Google menacent la democratie
items.digest: Cet essai Carnegie soutient que les grandes plateformes privees ont acquis un pouvoir politique sans responsabilite democratique equivalente. Il analyse comment les algorithmes, la moderation et des decisions produit opaques peuvent orienter le debat public presque autant que les controles mediatique d Etat. Le texte est retenu comme cadrage precoce du pouvoir des plateformes comme probleme de gouvernance.
